sentences.text: With this battalion, I was six month.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We start to lose people.
And, and we were involved in little battles and then started to shrink and shrink and shrink, the army.
They sent us-- we're a formal point.
We have-- in-- we-- they sent us, all of us, by-- the army was shrinking-- start small.
We lost a couple of tank.
We lost ammunition.
We was-- we have to step out and step out, and the Germans coming up and up.
And then they sent in Korosten-- in, in Groznyi.
And Groznyi, this is North Caucasus.
It was a point-- I don't know in English.
I am-- they are forming-- they forming new armies over there.
Now, they took out all of them.
And they were forming an army.
Instead, so many people, they're giving additional people.
And they're helping-- they gave us new uniforms.
So the uniforms were the wool.
And who's healthy-- and they sent us back to fight.
Reorganized--
--absolutely.
Yes.
It was in Groznyi.
And then, I came back to the-- I came to the second Ukrainian front, which I was very scared maybe [LAUGHS] they will find out what happened.
Of when I ran away, when I lost the, the, the--
Somebody stole my gun.
And I was scared.
It was in  the second Ukrainian front.
Now, in time for war, it's no big-- not big connection between--
--groups, not too much a connection.
Is-- this is what my luck--
Fear was.
Yeah.
And they sent me to the second Ukrainian front.
And they sent me in a tank division, which I was a soldier, a little boy, already older.
I cleaned the tank.
I washed the tank with a gun.
I was behind the tank.
Like I told you, the kids, they used.
They took a couple of cows and cattle.
They dressed us as shepherds.
We came out to the highways.
And we were watching.
And the Germans were not suspicious.
They figured it was local shepherds from the farms.
And--
And we gave information to the army.
I tell you the truth, we didn't  come close even to them.
I personally didn't come eye to eye.
I didn't come close to them.
When we are surveying, it can be around-- you know, they have binoculars.
They see movement, cows, and young little kids.
They didn't give attention.
Yeah.
I was in the 44th Division-- [NON-ENGLISH] Division.
No--
No, this is 1944.
I was injured.
In 1943, end of '43, I was injured.
Yes.
I was injured.
I was injured just accidentally.
They bombed the German.
And the wave from the bomb-- something hit me.
And I was injured here on my neck.
And the teeth over here-- four-- you know, right away, threw it out.
And they took me in hospital.
And I was in hospital in Baku, Baku.
This is Uzbekistan, as a-- is Republic of Russia.
And after I came home out from the hospital in 1944, they sent me in the officer school.
And I was a cadet.
I was in the hospital six months, maybe, yeah, six months, five and a half.
Good, very good, with feeling, lots of mercy.
We cannot-- this is-- was not complain.
No--
No, it was a hospital in Kavkaz.
This is-- there are usually black people over there, a Respublik-- dark brown people.
This is a, a Respublik.
They're looking like Filipino people-- the-- that-- the skin type-- their religion Muslim.
Yes.
Yes.
Then they sent me to Groznyi.
And in Groznyi was a military school academy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was studying in academy-- young officers-- a young lieutenant, which has graduated the youngest in my age.
And I received one star, like a young lieutenant in tank knowledge, which is like tank mechanic.
Now, usually in normal times, you have to spend at least two years to be a young lieutenant.
Now, in time for war, they make it-- they cooked up very quickly personnel for the army.
Very quickly, they prepared.
And this was-- for a young lieutenant, it was six months.
And after six month, they sent me back to the army.
So you were being trained as a tank officer, as a cadet?
Yes, I was a lieutenant-- young lieutenant, where you have lieutenant.
Then older lieutenant is three stars.
And then it's going to be captain.
This--
Yeah.
After the six-month-- as a matter of fact, then I already have two medals.
I was-- I received in the army.
And when it's a young guy with two medals, they was treat-- they treat me very well.
For fighting--
--bravery.
I didn't have a very high medals.
Why, the medals were divided.
They have in Russia very high medals, which I didn't have them.
They have [NON-ENGLISH].
I didn't have them.
And they have medals-- the Red Star, which was coming in three stages, which I didn't have them.
They have normal just for fighting, bravery for fighting.
And I was not a hero.
But they have medals-- it's a gold star, like hero.
I didn't have them.
This kind of people, like, they have the special medals-- they treat them special, you know, with special respect.
I didn't belong in this category.
My medals is regular medals for bravery.
One medal-- I have a good medal, strong, which, after the war, they paid money for the medals.
Is-- they're very-- the very high medals, they used to-- people received 45, 50, 60 ruble a, a month.
For my medal, the highest I received, 11.50.
Yes.
No, six.
All together.
And I have two--
In 1944, they sent me to fight.
I was in front to Brest, Brest Litovsk.
And we was fighting in Poland.
We had gone to Poland to cut a road through to Poland.
And we came to the Elbe-- in Germany, to the Elbe.
We didn't cross the Elbe.
We didn't make a pontoon.
The bridge was broken.
Now there-- the American Army came already from the other side of the Elbe, which the higher officers-- the high officers met each-- the other of the other side.
It's--
The American--
--officers, yeah, of the other side, which--
Elbe.
The, the, the, the lake is Elbe.
And I was not on the other side.
And then in 1945, the war was finished.
And they let me go.
No, we came up to the Elbe.
They have the [NON-ENGLISH], the Elbe, or the lake.
They call this the Elbe.
We came up, up to the Elbe.
And I didn't go far deep to Germany.
Why-- the American Army came from the other side in Germany and the English Army.
And we came, and the French came.
And we was from this side of the Elbe.
And we were standing over there.
We were staying over there.
And when the war was finished in 1945, the Germans capitulated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they, they don't need an army over there so much-- the old regular army over there.
And I was young.
And the older people, the very young people, they sent us back deep--
--to Russia, yeah.
And they asked me in case I wanted to stay in the army, to make a career in the army, which I refused.
I was a little bit tired from the army.
And I decided I want to be free to look for my parents.
To tell you the truth, I didn't have too far to go.
I didn't know where to go.
I tried to come back to the town where I'm born.
Kostopol, which I didn't-- I didn't find anybody from relatives.
I find out everybody was killed.
Yeah, where they're killed.
The grave they showed me, the local people.
And the way they killed them-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everything I find out.
And I decide to leave the town.
They killed them.
The German killed them.
They-- my grandmother, my grandfather-- their religion was very extreme religion people, Orthodox.
And they were very Orthodox people.
They took the rabbi, our rabbi.
And they told the rabbi to make a meeting and to tell the people that death came with God send them.
And everybody put the tallis, the people.
First, they took the man in tallisim, tallisim.
They put it in.
And they took him-- we have, in forest a little bit out of town, a, a, a building they used to kill cattle over there.
It was beside over there, they dig it out holes, graves, big graves.
And, and they shot them over there.
They shot them.
Yes.
Yes, over there.
When I came--
--I was talking to the local people.
And some of them was nice people.
And they told me what happened.
After I left, I think, maybe around two weeks, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the inquisition started.
After I found out-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] next, you know, like I  told you, the army offered me to be in the army to make a career in the army.
And they promised me to go to study more, which continued to study after the war to complete to be the same officers.
One star, you have to continue to go another six months to spend it.
And I decided I don't want to be a military person.
And I didn't have a trade, didn't have a qualification.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I have a little bit money.
And I was attending a concert in a circus.
And I saw a magician was performing.
And I would say, with God's help, I catched, you know, most-- a couple.
And I start to repeat it into work.
And I see I can make it.
It's nothing complicated.
And then I met another guy who was a performer.
And I told him, I am very interesting.
And I'm from the army.
I have no qualifications, no trade, nothing.
And he give me a couple secrets.
They were old secrets, what is old for him.
But it was new for me.
Well, he's not using them.
A couple books, so you put a little snake.
And you take out flowers from the other side.
Magic act, yeah.
And, and I came to a concert hall.
And they had groups they used to travel performing.
And I recommended myself-- I'm a performer.
And they saw my tricks, and they like.
And you need lots of guts not to be a performer, and not to ever dare even to go out on the stage.
I never was on the stage.
And I, I would say God help.
And with God's help-- and I became a performer.
A magician.
And then I start to develop my own tricks, my own numbers.
And I was working in the Moscow Philharmonic.
I was working in Leningrad Philharmonic.
I don't know.
It just came.
A fantasy was created.
And I-- my fantasies, I make it to work.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
Pardon me?
The equipment is you make a schematic.
And when you have a schematic, you came to have-- in the schematic, what you're making is connected with metal.
You're going to a tinsmith.
He will make you the little box.
And you show him the way to make it.
And according to the schematic, he's making this.
And then you're developing your number-- the way to sell it, the product.
It's the same thing like a salesman.
And the way you have to introduce this new number to the public.
--I, I want to say I was a performer self-made.
I was not educated in a school magician, which people didn't believe me after my performances.
They figured if I finished-- didn't finish nothing.
I did-- I saw one time in a film Houdinis with the chains, which I'm making this.
I started to make Houdini's number with the chains.
And, and they considered me a performer.
No, I used-- we used to work with a group.
I used to have an assistant to prepare for me everything for the numbers.
And I used to be on the stage 20 minutes, 25 minutes, which is lots of time when it's different kind of performers-- acrobatic numbers and--
I was with a--
I was with a circus.
And then I was-- in Philharmonic, I was working.
Keo.
Keo.
As a matter of fact, it's Jewish.
Keo, K-E-O, Keo.
Yeah.
You see, in the beginning, I was assistant with them.
And then I became independent by myself.
And when I was working for myself, we-- like, like a variety group, singer, accordion, different kind of variety group.
And I  have  my magic tricks.
My performance was 20 minutes in a stage, which you always have better paid.
The longer on you're on the stage, you get more paid.
And we was a group of 18 people, 20 people.
I am a [RUSSIAN], too-- [RUSSIAN].
[RUSSIAN].
This is-- you're laying down.
And you work with the feet.
You're turning over--
Yes.
Yeah, another person.
Yes, it's similar, a little bit connected.
I was working with them.
And then [RUSSIAN]-- I found out I'm not very talented.
And I saw people much better from me.
And I saw that I will never reach the talent what they have.
And I decided to step out from this and just magic.
This was what I make a living in Russia, up the whole way-- up to the-- up to 19-- 1955-- up to 1954.
No, you see, after the war, there was not too many-- the Russian didn't trust too many performers to go overseas.
No.
I was in Poland.
But Poland, Poland was communistic.
I was in Bucharest, in Romania.
It was communist.
They let us out for concerts.
You know, like, in the capitalist countries, no, I didn't travel.
You see, after I was-- something I say, I was involved with against the Russian law.
And it was a court-- witnesses-- what I say.
And this-- and what I say what was fighting, who was wrong, was not worded.
Anyway, I don't want to repeat it.
It's not important.
You see, the point is they gave me 25 years.
Yes.
And they sent me to Siberia.
This is in--
Or 1952, or something.
I can't-- I can't remember.
I have-- a matter of fact, I have a paper.
They let me out from jail.
I sent to--
--Siberia.
I was sent to Arkhangelsk, where the people-- where they have a lot of years, like, 25 years, 20 years.
They sent it deeper to Siberia, more security places.
Then they sent-- they sent me to Kolyma, Kolyma.
This is a place in Russia, in Siberia, and six month is daylight, and six month is dark.
You don't see-- you don't know when is day and when is-- the six month is dark.
And--
Yeah.
And six month, you can, in the middle of the night, read a book outside.
You don't need light.
This is-- was Kolyma.
I was over there.
It was-- in the, the beginning, they sent me over there to cut wood.
It was very hard work.
The first three months were very hard, big snows, cold.
And I never was used to, to physical work.
And I used to cut the big trees-- big work.
And I never did enough work to have better food.
I always was behind work, which food-- I received less like somebody else.
And--
And--
Then they find out I'm a performer.
And they put a hand in me.
And they give me a light job, a librarian, in jail-- in camp.
And I prepared concerts for the holidays in the camp.
Pardon me?
It was in Kolyma, Kolyma.
Kolyma, this is, a matter of fact, not far from Alaska from the other side.
By-- the first location, Arkhangelsk, was just a, a point--
--where they--
Yes.
The people were coming in from every-- each direction.
Over there, they are sorting up.
And when they see 25 years, in 5 years, I, I don't have to-- I have no right to live close to a big city.
In 10 years, I don't have the right to vote.
It's practically, like, 40 years they give me.
This kind of-- type of people-- they don't want to read exactly details.
I'm not a dangerous person.
No, they put in right away signature, uh-huh, Kolyma, Kolyma, Kolyma.
And--
That's right. they figured up I am extreme dangerous for their society.
And--
No, they figure up-- they figure up-- what kind of dangerous I am?
I never was a dangerous person.
I never kill nobody.
Pardon me?
The-- Kolyma.
What-- like I told you, over there was-- the first three months was tough.
I was working [SIGHS] in bush [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very hard.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a new climate for me and the food and everything take together-- a different kind of personality people.
I didn't like it.
And you have to live with them under one roof.
And this all-- that's the environment around me.
I wasn't used to this-- be a performer, actor in a different society, before a officer.
And right now, if you're showing around on the underworld, different kind of people, different kind of background.
Yes, yes, very harsh.
And, like, when they put me up later, I was the editor in the local paper.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I improved my situation.
I was the librarian.
And I'm-- I forming concerts.
And they was happy they had a person to organize the concerts.
And when it was coming Russian holidays, they're making concerts in the camps over there.
And after this, I felt much better.
I have good food-- I mean, good food-- I was not hungry.
And I didn't work hard.
I have the opportunity to learn myself and to lift up a little bit my education, what I couldn't catch up.
